text: We start in the middle of a heated debate about whether a county can keep the profit from selling a family home just because the owners owed a small tax bill.
That is the living constitutionalist, moments before our AI jurist panel kicks off its deliberation on Pung versus Isabella County for SPLIT DECISION.

Let us lay out the facts. Timothy Pung passed away, leaving behind a house in Michigan. The estate owed the county about two thousand dollars in back property taxes. The county seized the house and sold it at a public auction for seventy-six thousand dollars. But here is the catch. The county kept every single penny of that seventy-six thousand dollars. They refused to give the family the seventy-three thousand dollars left over after the tax debt was paid. The family sued, arguing the government cannot just keep the equity in your house to pay a tiny tax bill. The lower courts agreed the family should get the money left over from the auction price. But the courts said the family does not have a right to the difference between that cheap auction price and what the house was actually worth on the open market. The family took this all the way to the federal appeals court, which agreed with the lower court's split decision.

And that is where the real stakes come in. If the county can take a house for a two grand tax bill and pocket seventy-six grand, that is a massive windfall for the government and a devastating loss for the family. It turns your home equity into a slush fund for the county. It is a classic case of the government using a technicality to enrich itself at the expense of a grieving family. The legal question before our panel is whether the Fifth Amendment's rule against taking property, and the Eighth Amendment's rule against excessive fines, put a stop to this kind of government overreach.
Let us listen in as the pragmatist changes her mind, realizing the lower court's rule creates a giant loophole for local governments to profit off of tax sales.

So she is saying if the government only has to pay you the cheap auction price, they have a huge incentive to lowball the sale and keep the difference. She wants to use the actual market value instead, because it is a standard rule we already use everywhere else to figure out what a house is worth.
Next, the precedent maximalist wrestles with a century of local governments relying on the old rules, but ultimately decides the legal foundation is crumbling.

He is admitting that even though cities have been doing this for a hundred years, the old legal justification just does not hold up anymore. He is flipping his vote to throw out the lower court's decision because the historical argument for keeping the surplus money is fundamentally broken.
We get some real color here as the federalism hawk tries to argue this is just standard tax collection, not a taking of property, while the civil libertarian pushes back hard against that idea.

Basically, the civil libertarian is reminding everyone that the whole point of the Constitution is to stop the government from treating your private property as its own personal revenue stream. The federalism hawk is trying to say the county was just enforcing a lien, but the civil libertarian sees right through that label. You cannot just use a minor tax debt as an excuse to confiscate a family's entire financial investment in their home.
The precedent maximalist flips again, this time zeroing in on a specific past case that finally breaks his reliance on older decisions and forces his hand.

He is pointing out that the Supreme Court already tore down the old rule in a recent case. He cannot vote to uphold a legal theory the Court has already killed, so he has to switch his vote and side with the family.
Then the minimalist finds a very narrow, technical way to flip her vote without making a massive, sweeping new rule about property values across the country.

She is finding a tiny flaw in how the lower court handled the paperwork. It lets her throw out the decision and help the family without having to answer the massive constitutional question about how to calculate the true value of the property. It is a very cautious approach, typical of the minimalist, who hates making big sweeping changes to the law when a small technical fix will do the job.

The living constitutionalist and the process formalist clash over the core purpose of the property rules in the Constitution, debating whether it is about preventing poverty or just following old municipal habits.

The living constitutionalist is arguing that the whole point of the Takings Clause is to stop the government from leaving people destitute. And notice the originalist backing her up from the other direction. He says even back in the founding era, fair payment never meant whatever a fire-sale auction happens to fetch.
Finally, the originalist and the federalism hawk lock horns over who actually gets to define property rights in the first place, the state or the federal Constitution.

The originalist is making the final point that while states can define property rules, they cannot use those rules to erase the basic property rights the Constitution protects. The state cannot just invent a loophole to take your house and keep the profit. It is a strong finish to the deliberation, drawing a hard line in the sand about the limits of state power.

When the dust settled, our panel voted five to four to throw out the lower court's decision and side with the family. The pragmatist, precedent maximalist, federalism hawk, and process formalist ended up in the dissent, arguing the county should keep the money.

But here is the kicker. The actual Supreme Court ruled nine to nothing in favor of the family. Our panel got the final outcome exactly right, but they completely misread the room on the coalition. The real Court was completely unanimous. Our AI jurists were split right down the middle, missing the broad consensus entirely.

Which brings us to our season scoreboard. We have to be honest about this. Our deliberating panel is currently sitting at sixty-six point seven percent correct. They are losing to the silent jury at seventy-seven point four percent, and trailing the solo model at eighty-three percent.

It is a fascinating dynamic. When you let the AI agents argue with each other, they actually get more stubborn and miss the unanimous consensus that the real Court finds. The debate is making them less accurate, not more. They are so focused on their specific judicial philosophies that they fail to see the obvious middle ground.

Despite the scoreboard, the panel's rulings on our pending cases are officially on the record. We will see how they hold up when the real justices hand down their decisions. Thanks for listening to SPLIT DECISION.
